meta.title: Building Accessible Web Applications
meta.excerpt: Practical guide to implementing WCAG 2.1 AA compliance in modern React applications

props.body: Accessibility is not optional - it is a legal requirement in many jurisdictions and a business imperative. Building accessible applications from the start is significantly cheaper than retrofitting accessibility later.

props.text: Before diving into implementation, it is important to understand why accessibility matters beyond compliance. The numbers make a compelling case for investment.

features.title: UK Equality Act
features.description: Requires reasonable adjustments for disabled users accessing services

features.title: Americans with Disabilities Act
features.description: Applies to websites of public accommodations in the US

features.title: European Accessibility Act
features.description: EU-wide requirements coming into full effect in 2025

features.title: WCAG 2.1 AA
features.description: The international standard referenced by most regulations

props.text: The foundation of accessible web applications is semantic HTML. Using the correct elements provides built-in accessibility features that ARIA cannot replicate.

notIdealFor: Buttons made from spans or divs

props.code:
// BAD - Inaccessible
Submit
// GOOD - Accessible // BAD - No label association Email // GOOD - Properly labeled We will never share your email

props.title: The First Rule of ARIA
props.body: Do not use ARIA if you can use a native HTML element that provides the same functionality. Native elements have built-in keyboard handling, focus management, and screen reader support.

props.text: Keyboard users navigate with Tab, Shift+Tab, and arrow keys. Proper focus management ensures they can access all interactive content in a logical order.

steps.label: Visible Focus
steps.sublabel: All interactive elements must have visible focus indicators

steps.label: Logical Order
steps.sublabel: Tab order should follow visual reading order

steps.label: Focus Trapping
steps.sublabel: Modals should trap focus until dismissed

steps.label: Focus Restoration
steps.sublabel: Return focus to trigger element when closing modals

props.text: When native HTML semantics are insufficient, ARIA (Accessible Rich Internet Applications) attributes provide additional context to assistive technologies.

props.title: Essential ARIA Attributes
rows: Provides accessible name when text is not visible
Indicates if collapsible content is open
Hides decorative content from screen readers

props.text: Color contrast affects users with low vision, color blindness, and those in bright environments. WCAG requires minimum contrast ratios between text and background.

props.title: Do Not Rely on Color Alone
props.body: Never use color as the only way to convey information. Always provide an additional indicator such as text, an icon, or a pattern. This helps users with color blindness and ensures information is preserved when printed in grayscale.

props.text: A comprehensive accessibility testing strategy combines automated tools with manual testing and real user feedback.

steps.label: Automated
steps.sublabel: axe-core, Lighthouse, ESLint a11y plugin - catches ~30% of issues

steps.label: Manual
steps.sublabel: Keyboard-only navigation, zoom to 200%, high contrast mode

steps.label: User Testing
steps.sublabel: Include disabled users in usability testing sessions

props.body: Build accessibility in from the start - retrofitting is expensive and incomplete. Use semantic HTML as your foundation, add ARIA only when necessary, ensure proper focus management, and test with real assistive technologies.
